Back to Portfolio Alisa Marin Case Study
B2B · SaaS CRM Platform 2024 – 2025 UI/UX Designer
onpoint

A CRM platform for managing commercial offer requests, from intake to qualified offer, across every channel.

Overview

A CRM built for
commercial teams

onpoint is an internal B2B SaaS platform developed for a Romanian enterprise to centralise and process commercial offer requests from multiple acquisition channels: website, call center, email, and manual entry.

Before onpoint, teams were juggling spreadsheets, inboxes, and phone notes. Requests fell through the cracks. There was no consistent qualification process, no traceability, and no single source of truth.

My role was to design the full product experience: list view, intake flows, request detail interface, status workflows, and bulk import features.

🎯
My Role
UI/UX Designer, sole designer on the product, embedded in an agile team with PM, developers, and business stakeholders.
🔧
Deliverables
User flows, high-fidelity UI, interactive prototypes, design specs, and functional documentation review.
🤝
Collaboration
Worked directly with PMs on scope and requirements, and with devs for feasibility and QA sign-off.
🛠
Tools
Figma, FigJam, Material UI components. Weekly design reviews with stakeholders.
Problem Space

Scattered requests,
zero traceability

The client's commercial team received offer requests through at least four different channels with no unified system: website forms, phone calls, emails, and Excel files sent by partners. Each channel had its own workflow, or lack of one.

Agents had to manually track who was responsible for each request, what stage it was in, and whether a qualified offer had been created. Requests were duplicated, lost, or never followed up.

Beyond the chaos, the client also needed a formal qualification layer, differentiating between requests ready to become offers versus those needing more info or flagged as non-compliant.

Core Design Challenge

"How do we design a workflow that handles 4 entry channels, 3 qualification statuses, and multiple agent roles, without overwhelming users who just want to process requests quickly?"

🌐 Website
Auto-imported via API. No agent action at intake, but requires qualification
📞 Call Center
Agent-created in real time during call. High pressure, must be fast
📁 Excel Import
Bulk entry from partners. Needs validation with clear error visibility
✉️ Email
Manual entry from parsed email. Inconsistent data, high error rate
Core Design Decision

Designing the
qualification workflow

The most critical design challenge was the status model. Requests needed to move through stages in a controlled, traceable way, with the right actions available at each step and no way to accidentally advance or lose a request.

Request Status Flow
Necalificată
Entry state
all new requests
→
Calificată
Agent reviewed
+ assigned
→
Ofertă Draft
Offer being
prepared
→
Ofertă Finală
Completed &
sent
Refuzată
Rejected by agent
(reason required)
← available from Calificată
Neconformă
Terminal state
(no going back)
← available from any stage

A key insight was that "Neconformă" (non-compliant) needed to be a terminal state. Once marked, no further changes are possible. This was a business rule with legal implications, and the UI needed to communicate the irreversibility clearly without blocking legitimate workflows.

Screen: Requests List

The central
command view

The list view is where agents spend most of their time. It needed fast scanning, clear status signaling, and quick access to key actions without opening each request.

Cereri de ofertă · List View
01
Dual assignment state
Why it mattered
Status (Calificată/Necalificată) and assignment (Repartizată/Nerepartizată) were independent axes. A request could be qualified but unassigned, or assigned but still uncalified.
Decision Two separate badge columns, distinct visual language. Status = filled dot badges; Assignment = outlined badges. Both scannable at a glance.
02
Persistent filter bar
Why it mattered
Agents often return to the same filtered views repeatedly (e.g. "unassigned today" or "my team's qualified requests"). Filters that reset on navigation kill efficiency.
Decision Filters persist across navigation within the session and the active filter state is reflected in the URL for shareability.
Screen: New Request

Manual intake,
fast and structured

When agents receive a request by phone or email, they create it manually. The form needed to balance completeness with speed: the right fields visible, required ones clearly marked, and auto-fill from CUI lookup reducing manual typing.

Cerere nouă · Persoană juridică
Screen: Request Detail

Two panels,
one decision surface

The detail page was the most complex screen in the product. An agent needs to read request data, check comments, take action, and optionally edit, all in one context. The layout had to support both reading and doing without switching pages.

Gestionează cerere · Detail View
Calificată · Repartizată
Mod editare
Calificată · Nerepartizată
Necalificată · Repartizată
Tab Istoric evenimente
03
Split-panel architecture
Why it mattered
Agents need to see request data while deciding on an action. Putting data and actions in separate pages creates constant context-switching and cognitive friction.
Decision Left panel: request data (view/edit). Right panel: status, comments, and action buttons. Always visible together, no toggling needed.
04
Destructive action modals
Why it mattered
Actions like "Neconformă" are irreversible. "Refuză" re-routes the request. Both require explicit acknowledgment + reason selection to prevent accidents.
Decision Modal confirmation dialogs with mandatory reason dropdown, clear consequence text, and separated primary/destructive CTAs. "Neconformă" modal explicitly states it is terminal.
Screen: Bulk Import

Importing at scale,
without data blind spots

Partners would regularly send Excel files with dozens of request rows. The import feature needed to handle validation transparently, showing exactly which rows failed and why, rather than a generic "3 errors found" message.

Importă cerere ofertă
Stare inițială
Tab Valide
Tab Nevalide (erori inline)
05
Inline error highlighting
Why it mattered
When an import fails partially, users need to know exactly which cells failed and why, not just which row. A summary list of errors forces users to cross-reference with the source file.
Decision Invalid cells are highlighted red directly in the table. Users see the error in context. The "Nevalide (N)" tab count draws immediate attention on load.
06
Last file memory
Why it mattered
Agents often need to re-import after fixing errors in the source Excel. Returning to the import page and needing to re-upload was reported as a source of frustration.
Decision The "Ultimul fișier încărcat" section persists across sessions, allowing quick re-upload of a corrected version without navigating the file picker from scratch.
Impact

Measurable
before & after

After onpoint launched, the commercial operations team ran on the platform exclusively within the first month. Key efficiency gains were tracked through direct observations and stakeholder interviews.

4→1
Intake channels unified
All request sources now processed through a single interface
0
Duplicate-entry complaints
CUI-based deduplication eliminated manual double-checking
~40%
Faster request processing
From intake to qualified offer, measured across 3 pilot weeks
Reflections

What I
learned

  • 01
    Business rules are UX constraints. The qualification workflow had strict legal and operational rules that could not be bent for UX convenience. The real design skill was making compliant flows feel natural, not fighting the rules, but building around them.
  • 02
    Terminal states need extra communication investment. "Neconformă" being irreversible caused anxiety in testing. The solution was not to make it reversible, but to write better warning copy and design a modal that made the stakes legible before action.
  • 03
    Import flows reveal your data model's health. The bulk import screen exposed gaps in how the CRM handled nullable fields, conditional requirements, and cross-entity validation. Designing the error states forced us to audit the data model, which made the whole product more robust.
  • 04
    Dual-panel layouts require discipline. The split between form data and action panel worked, but only because we had clear rules about what belongs in each side. Scope creep in early iterations (e.g. putting the status breadcrumb in the form side) caused confusion until we codified the spatial logic.
